did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), a deep learning method, to classify skin lesions as melanoma or benign. The CNN was trained on the HAM10000 dataset using data augmentation (flips, rotations) and the Adam optimizer. The AI classification results, along with features derived from ABCD rules and photometric stereo, are visualized via an Augmented Reality mobile application to support dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original work proposing a new Augmented Reality mobile application for skin lesion data visualization. It describes the implementation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) classifier trained on the HAM10000 dataset, the extraction of features using ABCD rules and photometric stereo, and the development of the AR system on an iPhone. It includes a preliminary evaluation with 7 dermatologists, constituting an original technical and validation study rather than a review of existing literature.

title: An Augmented Reality Mobile Application for Skin Lesion Data Visualization
brief_summary: This study proposes a mobile Augmented Reality application to support dermatologists in the early diagnosis of melanoma by visualizing skin lesion data directly in the clinical environment. The system utilizes a Convolutional Neural Network trained on the HAM10000 dataset to classify lesions as melanoma or benign, while also extracting features based on the ABCD rules and a novel photometric stereo technique. The AR interface overlays these computed parameters and classification results onto the patient's skin via a mobile device camera. The approach aims to assist rather than replace dermatologists by providing comprehensive, real-time visual cues during examination. The paper describes the technical implementation of the deep learning model and the AR visualization system. It concludes by outlining the system's potential to enhance diagnostic accuracy through integrated data visualization.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the study. The CNN classifier was trained to identify melanoma. The dataset HAM10000 contained 1,113 melanoma images (augmented to 8,904). The paper also details clinical subtypes of melanoma in the background section.

The CNN classifier performed a binary classification of melanoma vs. benign. The HAM10000 dataset used for training included 6,705 images of melanocytic nevi and 1,099 images of benign lesions similar to keratosis. The paper notes that melanoma can be confused with other benign lesions.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions as melanoma or benign
all_task_types.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained on the HAM10000 dataset to classify skin lesions. The model uses a Sigmoid activation function in the final fully connected layer to produce a binary classification (melanoma vs. benign). The reported accuracy was 74.6%.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: Human Against Machine with 10000 training images, a public dataset containing 10,015 dermatoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used to train the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) classifier. The dataset was split into 80% for training and 20% for testing. Additionally, 15% of the training set was used for validation. Data augmentation (flips, rotations) was applied to the melanoma class to address imbalance, resulting in 8,904 augmented melanoma images.
use.validation: 15% of the initial training set was used for validation.
use.testing: 20% of the initial dataset was used for testing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The study describes the technical implementation of an AR mobile application and a CNN classifier, reporting an accuracy of 74.6% on the HAM10000 dataset. A preliminary evaluation with 7 dermatologists provided qualitative feedback (positive/very positive) but did not include statistical hypothesis testing.

HAM10000.notes: The CNN classifier was trained on the HAM10000 dataset (split into 80% training, 20% testing, with 15% of the training set used for validation). Data augmentation was applied to the melanoma class (flips, rotations) to address class imbalance. The model achieved an average accuracy of 74.6%.

architecture_details.structure: 4 convolutional blocks and one last block for classification

data_augmentation.method: Horizontal and vertical flips, rotations of 180, 90, and -90 degrees

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: Horizontal and vertical flips, rotations of 180, 90, and -90 degrees applied to melanoma images to address class imbalance.

Hair removal: Canny edge detection for segmentation of light and dark hair, refinement with morphological operators (closing), dilation for precision, and image inpainting (transport of coherence) to repair occluded areas.

Lesion segmentation: Conversion from RGB to grayscale, Otsu’s thresholding for clustering-based image thresholding to separate lesion from background, and median filtering to reduce over-segmentation and blurring.
Edge detection: Canny edge detection used for hair removal and lesion border identification.
Morphological operations: Closing and dilation operators applied during hair removal process.
Image inpainting: Used to reconstruct damaged parts of the digital image after hair removal.
Light intensity adjustment: Incremental activation of smartphone flashlight to ensure optimal lighting and unblurred images.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to melanoma images to address class imbalance.
Applied to melanoma images at angles of 180, 90, and -90 degrees to address class imbalance.

conclusions: The proposed Augmented Reality mobile application successfully integrates a Convolutional Neural Network classifier and feature extraction based on ABCD rules and photometric stereo to support dermatologists in the early diagnosis of melanoma.
The CNN classifier achieved an accuracy of 74.6% on the HAM10000 dataset for binary classification of melanoma versus benign lesions.
A preliminary evaluation with 7 dermatologists indicated that 6 out of 7 participants provided positive or very positive feedback regarding the usefulness of the AR visualization approach.
The system aims to assist rather than replace dermatologists by providing comprehensive, real-time visual cues directly in the clinical environment.

Future work includes adopting different deep learning techniques (e.g., residual neural networks) to improve accuracy, incorporating texture features, mitigating inaccuracies in size/distance computation, and including the Evolution parameter from the ABCDE rules.
important_remarks: The study is a preliminary evaluation with only 7 dermatologists, of whom 2 were already against the use of such technologies, limiting the generalizability of the positive feedback.
The CNN classifier achieved a relatively low accuracy of 74.6% on the HAM10000 dataset, which may impact clinical reliability.
The system relies on the 'Evolution' parameter (ABCDE rule) for future work, indicating it is currently missing from the implemented feature set.
The paper acknowledges inaccuracies in computing sizes and distances, which are critical for melanoma diagnosis, as a limitation to be addressed in future work.
The study does not report external validation on independent datasets, relying solely on the HAM10000 dataset for training and testing.
The approach is implemented on a specific hardware (iPhone 11 Pro Max) and software version (iOS 13.3.1), which may limit deployment compatibility.
The paper claims to be the first to use photometric stereo for melanoma feature extraction, but this is a novel technical contribution rather than a validated clinical improvement.

Data augmentation was applied only to the melanoma class to address imbalance, which may introduce bias if not balanced across other classes or if the augmentation does not reflect real-world variability.
